name: Favora
classification_notes: Explicitly designed as a display typeface, offered in two weights that reference commercial signage, protest posters and architectural lettering rather than text setting.
summary: A two-weight display typeface drawing on commercial signage and protest lettering, designed at In-House Intl for hopeful brand identities and climate campaigns.
description: The heavier weight references large-scale imprint posters and public announcement signage, while the lighter weight pulls from architectural engravings and formal advertising. Both cuts are drawn with deliberate warmth, described by the studio as slightly dusty and bright-eyed, lending characters a handmade quality suited to posters, packaging and digital billboards. Developed by Rodrigo Fuenzalida at FragType, the family has been featured in Slanted, PRINT Magazine and Behance Picks.
foundry_blurb: In-House describe Favora as an optimistic display typeface built for hopeful identities, climate campaigns, packaging, posters and digital displays. The two-weight family channels commercial and protest signage in its bolder cut, and sophisticated architectural lettering and advertising in its lighter counterpart, with both weights imbued with hope and a promise of progress.

name: Serrucho Variable Font
classification_notes: A variable display typeface with a single axis controlling the degree of serration across eight weights, from blocky and authoritative to spiked and frenetic. Clearly display category with no body-text application intended.
summary: A variable display typeface with a single axis that morphs blocky, angular letterforms into spiky, electrified shapes, built for posters, merch and loud branding.
description: Designed by Alexander Wright and Michu Benaim Steiner at In-House International, with type development by Rodrigo Fuenzalida at FragType, Serrucho runs a single variable axis across eight positions from flat and blocky to violently serrated. The name comes from the Spanish word for handsaw, and the aesthetic draws on post-punk flyers, hardcore posters and protest graphics. It suits applications where strong contrast and raw energy matter more than legibility at small sizes.
foundry_blurb: In-House describe Serrucho as a variable typeface with teeth, named after the handsaw, that morphs from controlled brutality to unleashed frenzy across eight weights. At its flattest it is bold and authoritative; at its most extreme, edges erupt into spikes for a mechanical and feral effect. They position it for loud branding, event graphics, packaging and headlines that refuse to be ignored.

name: Locha
designer: Alexander Wright, Michu Benaim Steiner, Rodrigo Fuenzalida
classification_notes: Locha is a softly geometric sans-serif with rounded counters and flowing connections, drawing on the circular warmth of 1970s signage lettering while maintaining contemporary versatility. The Slab and Soft subfamilies extend the family beyond strict sans-serif territory, but the core construction is geometric sans.
summary: A softly geometric sans in five stylistic variants, drawing on the rounded warmth of 1970s hand-painted signage for branding, editorial and environmental use.
description: Designed by Alexander Wright and Michu Benaim Steiner at In-House International, with type development by Rodrigo Fuenzalida, Locha takes its cues from the circular lettering of 1970s storefronts and public signage. Five styles cover the range from a straightforward Regular and a sturdy Slab to two diagonal variants that add rhythm and movement, plus a Soft cut for warmer applications. Extended Latin character support runs across all five styles, making it workable across a broad range of European languages.
foundry_blurb: In-House describe Locha as a warm, softly geometric typeface referencing 1970s signage culture, with rounded counters, generous spacing, and flowing connections that evoke hand-painted storefronts from an era when commerce meant community. Its five styles offer different interpretations of this character, from Regular and Slab for trustworthy branding to Diagonal variants for headline rhythm and a Soft version for approachable warmth. The foundry positions Locha as a response to the pace of optimisation culture, reclaiming presence, connection, and design at human scale.

name: Perfora
classification_notes: Perfora is a monospaced, brick-like display font with punch-hole shaped counters, available as a variable font with axes for width and height. Its primary use case is logos, posters and festival lineups, placing it firmly in the display category.
summary: A heavy, monospaced variable display font with punch-hole counters, designed to stretch across five widths and five heights for logos, posters and festival lineups.
description: Built by In-House International and designed by Alexander Wright, Perfora pairs the solidity of a brick-like mono construction with variable axes that let words expand taller or wider to fill any format. Two uppercase styles, rounded and angular, sit alongside 19 ornamental glyphs and stylistic alternates. Available as a single variable TTF or as 25 static OTF files spanning five widths and five heights.
foundry_blurb: In-House describe Perfora as a sturdy, flexible variable font built to stack, stretch and shape dynamic logos, posters, lineups and products. Named for its punch-hole shaped counters and eyes, it offers two uppercase styles, latin diacritics, stylistic alternates and 19 ornamental glyphs, and is available as a variable TTF or as 25 static fonts across five widths and five heights.

name: Zanco Variable Font
classification_notes: A narrow, expressive display typeface with stressed curves, unconventional weight distribution and art nouveau inflections; the variable axis and stilt-like proportions place it firmly in the display category rather than any conventional text classification.
summary: A narrow variable display typeface with stressed curves and unconventional weight distribution, designed by Alexander Wright at In-House Intl.
description: Designed by Alexander Wright and developed by Rodrigo Fuenzalida, Zanco runs from a slim, ethereal cut to a heavy-footed version where thickening shadows fill in the lower halves of letterforms, producing a melty, nostalgic quality. Its proportions are named after zancos, the stilts worn by acrobats, and the elongated, narrow stance lends it an art nouveau flavour at lighter weights. The variable axis makes it well suited to brand identities, packaging and poster work where a single font needs to carry across a range of sizes and moods.
foundry_blurb: In-House Intl describe Zanco as a celebration of delicious contradictions, balancing elegance and awkwardness, youth and sophistication in a single variable display font. Standing tall and narrow with stressed curves and softened internal angles, it moves from lanky to heavy across its variable axis, with interlocking descenders they call especially lovely at heavier weights. They pitch it for brand identities with heart, packaging, posters, billboards and motion graphics.

name: Brinca Variable Font
designer: Alexander Wright, Rodrigo Fuenzalida, Michu Benaim Steiner
classification_notes: A variable display typeface spanning from sharp compressed forms to bouncy rounded letters; its expressive, non-text-setting nature and extreme stylistic range place it firmly in display territory.
summary: A full-spectrum variable font shifting between sharp angular forms and rounded bouncy letters, designed for expressive brand and packaging work.
description: Designed by Alexander Wright, Rodrigo Fuenzalida and Michu Benaim Steiner, Brinca spans three distinct moods along a single variable axis: tight angular pleats, a neutral upright sans and inflated bouncy letterforms. Available as a single variable .ttf or five static OpenType files, it covers uppercase and lowercase Latin alphabets, numerals, punctuation and diacritics including Ñ. The wide stylistic range makes it a practical tool for brand systems, book covers, packaging and event promotions where visual variety is needed within a single typeface.
foundry_blurb: In-House Int'l describe Brinca as a full-spectrum variable font with emotional range and a dynamic heart, morphing from sharp tight pleats through an office-ready neutral sans into joyful, bouncy letters. Named after its jumping extremes, it is positioned as a versatile choice for expressive brand identities, packaging, events and merchandise, developed at FragType and available through YouWorkForThem.

name: Pata Slab
classification_notes: An ultra-heavy display slab serif with extreme weight contrast between thick stems and hairline centres, modular construction and no descenders, placing it firmly in the display slab category.
summary: An ultra-heavy display slab with hairline contrast centres, no descenders and a fixed-width modular grid that makes every character the same height and width.
description: Designed by Alexander Wright and Rodrigo Fuenzalida at In-House Intl in 2020, Pata Slab takes its name from a colloquialism for feet and builds upward from a chunky baseline with no descenders whatsoever. All uppercase characters sit within a square of identical width and height, while lowercase letters, numerals and punctuation follow the same height restrictions, creating a highly uniform modular system. The standard-height terminals allow for near-endless ligature combinations, making it well suited to stacked display headlines and poster layouts.
foundry_blurb: In-House describe Pata Slab as an ultra-heavy display font with chunky slabs, sharp contrast between thick strokes and hairline centres, and a modular construction in which every uppercase character fits precisely inside a square. With no descenders, all characters grow upward from the baseline. The design supports modular ligatures through its uniform terminal heights, making text layout and stacking straightforward.

name: Broker
classification_notes: Broker is categorised as a display typeface owing to its angular, modular construction and twelve variable styles ranging from mono-weight to stressed and serifed cuts, all built on a rectangular frame for poster and branding use rather than text setting.
summary: An angular variable display family built on a rectangular frame, shifting between slim mono-weight and chunky serifed styles via variable controls for thickness, decoration and shape.
description: Designed by Alexander Wright and In-House International and developed by Rodrigo Fuenzalida at FragType, Broker draws on DIY cut-paper lettering to produce something deliberately rough and expressive. Twelve styles are accessible across a variable axis controlling thickness, decoration and shape, moving from square mono-weight through stressed angled cuts to slab-heeled display weights. The modular rectangular frame makes stacking and grid-based layouts straightforward, lending the family to posters, packaging, cover art and motion work.
foundry_blurb: In-House describe Broker as an angular variable display type family built for carefree experimentation but designed to make a statement. With twelve unique styles and variable controls for thickness, decoration and shape, it can shift from slim square mono-weight to edgy stressed angles and chunky serif heels. Drawn on a rectangular, modular frame, it suits posters, funky packaging, flyers, cover art, motion displays and fearless branding, and is available as both a variable TTF and thirteen individual OpenType fonts.

name: Snare
designer: Alexander Wright, In-House International, Rodrigo Fuenzalida
classification_notes: Snare is a display typeface with stencil-like angled cuts and variable heights, giving it a distinctly expressive, experimental character that places it firmly in the display category.
summary: A stencil-inflected variable display typeface with angled cuts and shifting character heights that create a syncopated, drumbeat rhythm across words.
description: Designed by Alexander Wright and In-House International, Snare uses angled section breaks and variable heights to give each character its own internal logic while keeping its footprint constant. As heights shift, unique crossbars, periscoping capitals and deep descenders emerge, producing a visual rhythm that grows more pronounced the more extreme the settings. The family ships in ten standard weight-as-height variants in OTF and as a variable TTF, with one uppercase alphabet and two lowercase variants.
foundry_blurb: In-House describe Snare as a jazzy little display type that presents like a stencil but behaves in its own way. Featuring angled section breaks and variable heights, it keeps each character's footprint steady while revealing unique crossbars, periscoping capitals and deep-sinking descenders. Beyond being playful and useful for tight spaces, they note its lanky verticals and nervous energy reflect the uncertainty of its creation during the second pandemic spring of 2021.

name: Maquinista
classification_notes: Maquinista is a strictly display typeface with layered, prismatic letterforms built from hard geometry and striped depth, drawing on 1970s Avant Garde and Venezuelan geometric design traditions. Its dimensional construction and retro-pop character place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A prismatic display typeface by Alexander Wright drawing on 1970s Avant Garde geometry and Venezuelan graphic design, built for short bold headlines at large scale.
description: Layered letterforms with striped depth and angular construction give single words the graphic weight of signage, record sleeves and poster type. The design references Avant Garde and the structured geometric visual art of Venezuela, particularly the work of Gerd Leufert. Best used in large display settings where colour, shadow and pattern can amplify each character's dimensional quality.
foundry_blurb: In-House describe Maquinista as a retro-futurist experimental font with curved lines and motifs, designed strictly for short headers at large scale. They cite its influences as the aesthetic vernacular of the 1970s, Avant Garde, and the structured geometric visual artwork made in Venezuela during that period, particularly the work of Gerd Leufert.

name: Troptical
classification_notes: Troptical is a display typeface built from concentric lines and angular, modular construction, drawing on op-art geometry and Latin American design traditions. Its kinetic, stripes-based aesthetic places it firmly in the display category with no meaningful connection to text type conventions.
summary: A multilinear display typeface inspired by op-art and Latin American geometry, built from concentric lines and modular construction across 45 fonts.
description: Designed by Alexander Wright for In-House, Troptical draws on the op-art scene and geometric design traditions of Caracas, using concentric stripes and angular letterforms to generate visual rhythm and movement. The family spans three styles (standard, square and round) across five widths and three weights, with character widths standardised across styles so that mixing and matching produces consistent optical effects. Each style is also available as a variable font, and the glyph set includes Spanish diacritics including the eñe.
foundry_blurb: In-House describe Troptical as a hypnotic, energetic multilinear typeface inspired by op-art dynamism and Latin American design, with a nod to 8-bit video game playfulness. They position it as their second typeface release and a statement of foundry direction: unapologetically exuberant display typefaces built for logos, graphic headers, packaging, apparel and outdoor signage.

name: Ragtag Display
classification_notes: Ragtag is a geometric display typeface with three interchangeable glyph variants per letterform, designed explicitly for expressive, loud display use rather than text setting.
summary: A geometric display font with three interchangeable glyph variants per letter, designed for adventurous branding and poster work.
description: Designed by Alexander Wright and digitised by Rodrigo Fuenzalida at In-House International, Ragtag offers three distinct glyph sets for each character across the full alphabet and numerals, allowing designers to mix and match letterforms within a single word or composition. Each character is drawn so it reads as a self-contained graphic unit while still composing harmoniously with the rest. Spanish diacritics and eñes extend its usability beyond standard Latin, and the font ships in OpenType format for both Mac and PC.
foundry_blurb: In-House describe Ragtag as fun, graphic and loud, with three irreverent geometric variations per letterform designed so each character could stand alone as a logo while still composing beautifully within a word. Inspired by the scattered crew of makers at their Austin and Barcelona studio, the font is built for adventurous branding, posters and expressive type, and includes eñes and diacritics for Spanish-language use.

name: Alicia
classification_notes: Built entirely from three elemental shapes — triangle, circle and rectangle — Alicia is a display typeface with a modular, shape-based construction that prioritises graphic presence over text legibility.
summary: A geometric display typeface by Alexander Wright, constructed entirely from triangles, circles and rectangles for expressive, shape-led lettering.
description: Each letter is built from just three forms — triangle, circle and rectangle — giving the family a modular rhythm while preserving the individual character of each glyph. The shape-based system reads quickly at large sizes, making it well-suited to posters, event graphics and identity work where the letterforms need to register before they are read closely. A third variation exists for the most frequently used letters in English, adding visual nuance to the core system.
foundry_blurb: In-House describe Alicia as a light-hearted, playful font based on geometrical forms, in which all letters, punctuation marks and numbers are constructed from only three shapes: triangle, circle and rectangle. They note that this approach celebrates the diversity and individual personality of each letter without losing its relation to the whole family, and describe it as an experimental typeface with great contrast between letterforms, ideal for attention-grabbing designs.
